item.description:

item.title:
IRAN-LINKED TANKERS SURGE THROUGH HORMUZ
Shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has nearly stalled, but some traffic continues — led increasingly by Iranian-linked vessels.
A Pakistan-flagged Aframax tanker carrying crude cleared the strait Sunday and is heading to Karachi, according to Bloomberg tracking data.
Iran-linked passages jumped to 12 ships in the past 24 hours, a wartime high, including a VLCC bound for China.
item.description:
IRAN-LINKED TANKERS SURGE THROUGH HORMUZ

Shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has nearly stalled, but some traffic continues — led increasingly by Iranian-linked vessels.

A Pakistan-flagged Aframax tanker carrying crude cleared the strait Sunday and is heading to Karachi, according to Bloomberg tracking data.

Iran-linked passages jumped to 12 ships in the past 24 hours, a wartime high, including a VLCC bound for China.

item.title: IRANIAN FOREIGN MINISTER SAYS REPORTS CLAIM SOME 'NEIGHBORING STATES' WHICH HOST U.S. FORCES AND PERMIT ATTACKS ON IRAN ARE ALSO ACTIVELY ENCOURAGING KILLINGS
item.description: IRANIAN FOREIGN MINISTER SAYS REPORTS CLAIM SOME 'NEIGHBORING STATES' WHICH HOST U.S. FORCES AND PERMIT ATTACKS ON IRAN ARE ALSO ACTIVELY ENCOURAGING KILLINGS

item.title:
SPAIN’S SÁNCHEZ SAYS TRUMP IRAN WAR UNDERMINES GLOBAL ORDER
Pedro Sánchez accused Donald Trump of weakening the international order by launching a unilateral war with Iran without consulting allies.
The Spanish prime minister said the conflict risks damaging the global system and warned that policies from Washington could erode welfare systems and hurt middle- and working-class citizens.
Tensions between Spain and the U.S. have also grown over defense spending and the war. Madrid has refused to meet a proposed 5% of GDP NATO defense target and blocked the use of Spanish bases for operations linked to the Iran conflict.
Despite the dispute, Sánchez said allies should remain honest with one another and maintain a strong transatlantic relationship.
item.description:
SPAIN’S SÁNCHEZ SAYS TRUMP IRAN WAR UNDERMINES GLOBAL ORDER

Pedro Sánchez accused Donald Trump of weakening the international order by launching a unilateral war with Iran without consulting allies.

The Spanish prime minister said the conflict risks damaging the global system and warned that policies from Washington could erode welfare systems and hurt middle- and working-class citizens.

Tensions between Spain and the U.S. have also grown over defense spending and the war. Madrid has refused to meet a proposed 5% of GDP NATO defense target and blocked the use of Spanish bases for operations linked to the Iran conflict.

Despite the dispute, Sánchez said allies should remain honest with one another and maintain a strong transatlantic relationship.

item.title: U.S. STOCKS EXTEND GAINS, NASDAQ UP 1.00%
item.description: U.S. STOCKS EXTEND GAINS, NASDAQ UP 1.00%

item.title:
YARDENI WARNS CONTRARIAN BUY SIGNAL MAY FAIL
Market pessimism is usually a classic buy signal, but veteran strategist Ed Yardeni says this time could be different.
The S&P 500 has fallen nearly 5% from its January peak as the Iran war keeps oil prices elevated and threatens shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. While weak sentiment typically creates buying opportunities, Yardeni says policymakers may have limited ability to offset economic damage from a prolonged conflict.
That uncertainty is making many strategists more cautious. Oil near $100 a barrel could trigger a broader stock correction, analysts at firms including Morgan Stanley, JPMorgan Chase and Evercore ISI warn.
For now, Yardeni says the geopolitical risks make it harder than usual to confidently “buy the dip.”
item.description:
YARDENI WARNS CONTRARIAN BUY SIGNAL MAY FAIL

Market pessimism is usually a classic buy signal, but veteran strategist Ed Yardeni says this time could be different.

The S&P 500 has fallen nearly 5% from its January peak as the Iran war keeps oil prices elevated and threatens shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. While weak sentiment typically creates buying opportunities, Yardeni says policymakers may have limited ability to offset economic damage from a prolonged conflict.

That uncertainty is making many strategists more cautious. Oil near $100 a barrel could trigger a broader stock correction, analysts at firms including Morgan Stanley, JPMorgan Chase and Evercore ISI warn.

For now, Yardeni says the geopolitical risks make it harder than usual to confidently “buy the dip.”

item.title:
🚨 TRUMP TO HOLD WHITE HOUSE NEWS CONFERENCE
Donald Trump said he will hold a news conference at the White House on Monday.
In a post on Truth Social, Trump said the briefing will take place before his 11:45 a.m. closed-door meeting with the board of the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts.
He did not specify the topic of the news conference.
item.description:
🚨 TRUMP TO HOLD WHITE HOUSE NEWS CONFERENCE

Donald Trump said he will hold a news conference at the White House on Monday.

In a post on Truth Social, Trump said the briefing will take place before his 11:45 a.m. closed-door meeting with the board of the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts.

He did not specify the topic of the news conference.

item.title:
MIDDLE EAST WAR THREATENS GLOBAL CHIP SUPPLY
The ongoing Middle East war is raising new risks for the global semiconductor industry, with potential disruptions to key chipmaking supplies and energy flows.
Taiwan — home to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company — relies heavily on imports of materials like helium and sulfur, as well as energy from the Middle East. Any disruption around the Strait of Hormuz could push up power costs or restrict critical inputs for chip production.
TSMC produces about 90% of the world’s most advanced chips, including processors for Nvidia and Apple. Supply problems could therefore ripple across industries from AI and data centers to cars and consumer electronics.
Taiwan is particularly vulnerable because it imports 97% of its energy and keeps only about 11 days of LNG reserves, leaving its chip industry exposed if the conflict disrupts energy shipments.
item.description:
MIDDLE EAST WAR THREATENS GLOBAL CHIP SUPPLY

The ongoing Middle East war is raising new risks for the global semiconductor industry, with potential disruptions to key chipmaking supplies and energy flows.

Taiwan — home to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company — relies heavily on imports of materials like helium and sulfur, as well as energy from the Middle East. Any disruption around the Strait of Hormuz could push up power costs or restrict critical inputs for chip production.

TSMC produces about 90% of the world’s most advanced chips, including processors for Nvidia and Apple. Supply problems could therefore ripple across industries from AI and data centers to cars and consumer electronics.

Taiwan is particularly vulnerable because it imports 97% of its energy and keeps only about 11 days of LNG reserves, leaving its chip industry exposed if the conflict disrupts energy shipments.

item.title:
IRAN WAR INFLATION SHOCK MOSTLY LIMITED TO ENERGY
Central banks meeting this week will gauge how much the Iran war could fuel inflation. But analysts at Goldman Sachs say the shock is far narrower than the post-Covid surge because it is largely concentrated in energy.
Higher oil prices could cut global GDP by about 0.3% and lift headline inflation by roughly 0.5 percentage points over the next year.
Disruptions around the Strait of Hormuz may also affect chemicals and metals, but current prices suggest only a 0.1-point increase in global inflation.
Goldman notes the Middle East accounts for just 1% of global non-energy exports, far less than the more than 20% of global trade disrupted by pandemic shutdowns in Asia.
item.description:
IRAN WAR INFLATION SHOCK MOSTLY LIMITED TO ENERGY

Central banks meeting this week will gauge how much the Iran war could fuel inflation. But analysts at Goldman Sachs say the shock is far narrower than the post-Covid surge because it is largely concentrated in energy.

Higher oil prices could cut global GDP by about 0.3% and lift headline inflation by roughly 0.5 percentage points over the next year.

Disruptions around the Strait of Hormuz may also affect chemicals and metals, but current prices suggest only a 0.1-point increase in global inflation.

Goldman notes the Middle East accounts for just 1% of global non-energy exports, far less than the more than 20% of global trade disrupted by pandemic shutdowns in Asia.

item.title:
INDIAN LPG TANKERS SAFELY CROSS STRAIT OF HORMUZ
Two India-flagged LPG tankers have safely crossed the Strait of Hormuz and are heading to India, marking a rare passage through the increasingly risky waterway.
The Shivalik is expected to reach India on Monday, while the Nanda Devi will berth early Tuesday, according to a senior Indian shipping official. Both vessels crossed the strait early Saturday and were not escorted by the Indian Navy.
The transit followed a recent call between Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi and Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian, and came after India helped repatriate more than 140 Iranian nationals from an Iranian vessel.
item.description:
INDIAN LPG TANKERS SAFELY CROSS STRAIT OF HORMUZ

Two India-flagged LPG tankers have safely crossed the Strait of Hormuz and are heading to India, marking a rare passage through the increasingly risky waterway.

The Shivalik is expected to reach India on Monday, while the Nanda Devi will berth early Tuesday, according to a senior Indian shipping official. Both vessels crossed the strait early Saturday and were not escorted by the Indian Navy.

The transit followed a recent call between Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi and Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian, and came after India helped repatriate more than 140 Iranian nationals from an Iranian vessel.

item.title:
MARKETS MAY BE UNDERPRICING A PROLONGED MIDDLE EAST WAR
Investors may be underestimating the risk that the Middle East conflict lasts longer than expected. According to Bank of America strategist Antonio Gabriel, while a quick resolution is possible, the conflict extending into the second quarter is just as likely.
Current U.S. stock valuations and expectations for the Federal Reserve suggest markets are not fully accounting for more disruptive outcomes for global growth. Meanwhile, money markets are already pricing in a full Fed rate cut by December, according to LSEG data.
item.description:
MARKETS MAY BE UNDERPRICING A PROLONGED MIDDLE EAST WAR

Investors may be underestimating the risk that the Middle East conflict lasts longer than expected. According to Bank of America strategist Antonio Gabriel, while a quick resolution is possible, the conflict extending into the second quarter is just as likely.

Current U.S. stock valuations and expectations for the Federal Reserve suggest markets are not fully accounting for more disruptive outcomes for global growth. Meanwhile, money markets are already pricing in a full Fed rate cut by December, according to LSEG data.

item.title:
US–CHINA TRADE TALKS IN PARIS SHOW PROGRESS
U.S. and Chinese officials held “candid and constructive” talks in Paris, agreeing to work toward greater stability in their trade relationship, according to sources familiar with the discussions.
Both sides discussed possible increases in Chinese purchases of U.S. agricultural goods ahead of the upcoming Trump–Xi summit in Beijing. U.S. officials also raised the need for China to buy more Boeing aircraft, coal, oil, and gas.
The talks explored managed trade and investment mechanisms for leaders to consider at the summit, and addressed U.S. firms’ difficulties obtaining rare earths such as yttrium.
Technical-level negotiations will continue Monday to refine the details.
item.description:
US–CHINA TRADE TALKS IN PARIS SHOW PROGRESS

U.S. and Chinese officials held “candid and constructive” talks in Paris, agreeing to work toward greater stability in their trade relationship, according to sources familiar with the discussions.

Both sides discussed possible increases in Chinese purchases of U.S. agricultural goods ahead of the upcoming Trump–Xi summit in Beijing. U.S. officials also raised the need for China to buy more Boeing aircraft, coal, oil, and gas.

The talks explored managed trade and investment mechanisms for leaders to consider at the summit, and addressed U.S. firms’ difficulties obtaining rare earths such as yttrium.

Technical-level negotiations will continue Monday to refine the details.
